spec_match.rationale: Code block claims `rec.start(e.track)` is the new shape, but the spec defines `audioTrack` as a property. Worse: track #2 is a fake timer that prints canned phrases — no second recognizer actually runs, so the headline `one SpeechRecognition per remote track` claim is only half-demonstrated.

interactive.rationale: Track #1 wires a real recognizer to the mic; track #2 is a `setInterval` that paints canned strings. The user sees activity but there's no second SpeechRecognition instance actually consuming a second track.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises `two simulated participants caption simultaneously` and the demo does show two captioning lanes — but only one is a real SR; the other is faked text. The framing oversells what's wired up.

edge_cases.rationale: No handling shown for: a track that ends mid-call, a recognizer that errors (only logs the error code), the case where Chrome rejects the track overload, or the case where more than two participants join. The multi-track scaling story is the whole point and it's not exercised.

design_system.rationale: Variables only, two-tone pill states, no static-card pattern, mobile responsive grid.

openQuestions.title: Wire a real second recognizer instead of a setInterval fake
openQuestions.detail: The headline use case is `one SR per participant track`. Right now only track #1 has a real recognizer; track #2 paints `bobPhrases[i]` on a timer. Either use an `OscillatorNode → MediaStreamDestination` to generate a real track + recognizer pair, or pull from an `<audio>` element via `captureStream()`. Without this the demo doesn't actually prove the spec capability — it just claims to.

openQuestions.title: Reconcile `rec.start(track)` vs `rec.audioTrack = track`
openQuestions.detail: The code block uses `rec.start(e.track)`. The multi-source-captions sibling uses `rec.audioTrack = track`. One is right; the other is wrong. Read the WICG spec PR and the Chrome 135 intent-to-ship, then update.

openQuestions.title: Scale to N participants
openQuestions.detail: Two is a contrived case. Real conferences have 4-20 attendees. Add an `add participant` button that spawns a new lane + recognizer per click, tied to a synthesised audio source. The scaling cost (CPU? memory? quota?) is what authors actually want to know.

openQuestions.title: Handle track ending and recognizer error
openQuestions.detail: Add a `kick track #2` button that calls `track.stop()` and shows what happens to the recognizer (does `onend` fire? does it freeze?). Right now error logs go to a side panel that's easy to miss.

summary: Demo claims to caption two participants but only one lane runs a real recognizer; the other is a timer-driven text fake. That undermines the headline use case. Fix by generating a synthetic audio track for the second source, and reconcile the `start(track)` vs `audioTrack` API shape with the spec.
